clinicalStem: A 24-year-old woman presents with a 3-year history of recurrent sneezing, bilateral nasal congestion, watery rhinorrhoea, and itchy eyes. Symptoms are worst in spring and early summer. She has a personal history of eczema. Examination reveals pale, boggy inferior turbinates bilaterally and a transverse nasal crease. Her nasal smear shows numerous eosinophils. Which single investigation would BEST confirm the underlying diagnosis?
options: CT scan of the paranasal sinuses
Skin prick test with common aeroallergens
explanation: Skin prick testing (SPT) with common aeroallergens is the gold-standard first-line investigation to confirm IgE-mediated (allergic) sensitisation in a patient with a clinical picture of seasonal allergic rhinitis. A wheal ≥3 mm compared to negative control is a positive result and identifies the specific trigger. Total IgE is non-specific and does not identify the causative allergen. CT sinuses is indicated for suspected chronic rhinosinusitis, not primary diagnostic workup of rhinitis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Serum total IgE is often elevated in atopic individuals but is non-specific; it does not identify the causative allergen and can be normal in confirmed allergic rhinitis.
CT paranasal sinuses is the gold-standard for evaluating chronic rhinosinusitis and sinonasal anatomy, not for diagnosing allergic rhinitis.
Skin prick testing directly identifies the sensitising allergen and is the preferred first-line confirmatory test — this is the correct answer.
Nasal endoscopy assesses structural abnormalities and mucosal disease but does not confirm an allergic aetiology.
A full blood count may show peripheral eosinophilia in atopic disease but is non-specific and does not confirm allergic rhinitis.
clinicalPearl: Skin prick testing is the first-line investigation for confirming IgE-mediated allergic rhinitis; a transverse nasal crease ('allergic salute') is a classic exam finding.
keyClue: Seasonal pattern, personal atopy, eosinophils on nasal smear, pale boggy turbinates — all point to allergic rhinitis requiring allergen identification.
commonExamTrap: Choosing serum total IgE — it is elevated in atopy but is non-specific and does not confirm the causative allergen.
missedConcept: Distinction between non-specific atopy markers (total IgE, eosinophilia) and specific allergen-identification tests (SPT, specific IgE/RAST).

clinicalStem: A 52-year-old male office worker presents with a 4-year history of persistent nasal congestion, clear rhinorrhoea, and postnasal drip. Symptoms worsen on exposure to strong perfumes, cold air, and changes in weather. He denies itchy eyes or sneezing. Skin prick testing to a standard panel of 20 aeroallergens is entirely negative. Nasal smear reveals no eosinophils. Serum specific IgE to common aeroallergens is undetectable. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Vasomotor (idiopathic) non-allergic rhinitis presents with chronic nasal congestion and rhinorrhoea triggered by environmental stimuli (temperature changes, strong odours, humidity) without evidence of IgE-mediated allergy (negative SPT, undetectable specific IgE) and without nasal eosinophilia. NARES also shows negative allergy tests but characteristically has abundant nasal eosinophils on smear. Allergic rhinitis requires positive allergy testing.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Seasonal allergic rhinitis requires positive IgE-mediated sensitisation; allergy testing here is entirely negative, excluding this diagnosis.
NARES (non-allergic rhinitis with eosinophilia syndrome) has negative allergy tests but is defined by marked nasal eosinophilia, which is absent here.
Vasomotor non-allergic rhinitis fits perfectly: negative allergy tests, no eosinophilia, triggers are physical/chemical stimuli — this is the correct answer.
Allergic fungal rhinosinusitis typically shows eosinophilia, elevated IgE, positive fungal sensitisation, and CT evidence of sinus disease.
Nasal polyps may cause obstruction but would be visible on examination; this presentation describes rhinitis symptoms without mention of polyps.
clinicalPearl: Non-allergic rhinitis = chronic nasal symptoms + negative allergy testing; vasomotor subtype is triggered by physical stimuli and has no nasal eosinophilia.
keyClue: Triggers are physical stimuli (cold air, perfumes), no itchy eyes or sneezing, negative SPT and specific IgE, and absent nasal eosinophils.
commonExamTrap: Confusing vasomotor rhinitis with NARES — both are non-allergic, but NARES is characterised by eosinophilia on nasal smear.
missedConcept: The classification of non-allergic rhinitis subtypes and that negative allergy testing is required before labelling a patient as non-allergic.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old non-smoking man presents with a 14-month history of bilateral nasal obstruction, anosmia, and persistent mucopurulent discharge. He has a background of adult-onset asthma. Anterior rhinoscopy reveals bilateral pale glistening masses in the middle meatus. He has failed two courses of antibiotics and three months of intranasal corticosteroids. Which investigation is MOST appropriate to guide surgical planning?
options: Plain X-ray of the paranasal sinuses
Non-contrast CT scan of the paranasal sinuses
MRI of the brain and paranasal sinuses
explanation: Non-contrast CT scan of the paranasal sinuses is the gold-standard investigation for chronic rhinosinusitis (CRS) requiring surgical planning. It defines the extent of disease, sinonasal anatomy, and critical landmarks for endoscopic sinus surgery (ESS). Plain X-rays are inadequate for soft-tissue and anatomical detail. MRI is reserved for suspected intracranial extension, orbital complications, or soft-tissue tumour. RAST panels assess allergy, not surgical anatomy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Serum IgE and RAST assess allergic sensitisation, which does not guide surgical planning for CRS with nasal polyps.
Plain X-ray of the paranasal sinuses has poor sensitivity and specificity for CRS and is insufficient for pre-surgical anatomical assessment.
Non-contrast CT paranasal sinuses is the gold-standard for evaluating CRS extent and planning endoscopic sinus surgery — this is the correct answer.

MRI is superior for soft-tissue characterisation and is indicated when intracranial extension, orbital involvement, or neoplasm is suspected, but it is not the primary pre-operative investigation for routine CRS.
Nasal cytology may show eosinophils but does not provide anatomical information for surgical planning.
clinicalPearl: CT paranasal sinuses (non-contrast) is mandatory before endoscopic sinus surgery for CRS; it delineates critical anatomy and disease extent.
keyClue: 14 months of bilateral symptoms, bilateral polyps on rhinoscopy, failed medical therapy — this patient needs surgical planning, requiring anatomical CT.
commonExamTrap: Choosing MRI — MRI is superior for soft tissue but CT is preferred pre-operatively as it clearly shows bony landmarks critical for safe ESS.
missedConcept: The role of CT versus MRI in sinonasal disease: CT for bony anatomy and pre-op planning; MRI when soft-tissue detail or intracranial/orbital extension is suspected.

clinicalStem: A 9-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with a 5-day history of right-sided facial pain, nasal discharge, and fever. Today his mother noticed swelling and redness around his right eye. On examination: temperature 38.9°C, right periorbital erythema and oedema, proptosis, limited and painful right eye movements in all directions, and visual acuity is reduced in the right eye (6/18 vs 6/6 left). Which is the MOST appropriate NEXT investigation?
options: Nasal swab for culture and sensitivity
Urgent contrast-enhanced CT scan of the orbits and paranasal sinuses
explanation: This child has signs of orbital cellulitis with possible subperiosteal or orbital abscess: proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, and reduced visual acuity. This is a sight- and life-threatening emergency. Urgent contrast-enhanced CT of the orbits and sinuses is required to differentiate between preseptal cellulitis, orbital cellulitis, subperiosteal abscess, and orbital abscess — findings that directly dictate whether surgical drainage is needed. Any delay in diagnosis risks irreversible vision loss or intracranial spread.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Nasal swab guides antibiotic choice but does not assess the extent of orbital involvement; it cannot differentiate abscess from cellulitis.
Plain X-ray provides inadequate detail for orbital and soft-tissue assessment and would significantly delay diagnosis in a potentially sight-threatening emergency.
Contrast CT orbits and sinuses is the investigation of choice here — it is urgent and directly guides the need for surgical drainage — this is the correct answer.
Serum inflammatory markers confirm infection but do not localise or characterise the orbital complication; they cannot guide surgical decision-making.
Nasal endoscopy under GA may be part of surgical management but is not the immediate diagnostic step; CT must precede surgical intervention.
clinicalPearl: Proptosis + ophthalmoplegia + reduced visual acuity in a child with sinusitis = orbital abscess until proven otherwise — urgent contrast CT orbits/sinuses is mandatory.
keyClue: Proptosis, restricted and painful eye movements, and reduced visual acuity distinguish orbital from preseptal (periorbital) cellulitis and signal abscess formation.
commonExamTrap: Treating as simple preseptal cellulitis and omitting imaging — preseptal involves swelling anterior to the orbital septum only and does NOT cause proptosis or ophthalmoplegia.
missedConcept: The Chandler classification of orbital complications of sinusitis and the indications for urgent CT and surgical drainage.
redFlagReason: Orbital abscess is sight-threatening (optic nerve compression → irreversible blindness) and life-threatening (intracranial spread → meningitis/brain abscess). Delayed diagnosis is catastrophic.

subtopic: Site of bleeding and first-line management
clinicalStem: A 19-year-old university student attends his GP after three episodes of spontaneous nose bleeds over the past two months. Each episode involves bright red blood from the left nostril, is self-limiting within 5 minutes, occurs in winter, and is not associated with trauma. He is otherwise healthy, takes no medications, and has no family history of bleeding disorders. Anterior rhinoscopy reveals a prominent vascular area on the anterior nasal septum with superficial dilated vessels. Which is the MOST likely bleeding site?
options: Posterior lateral nasal wall (sphenopalatine artery territory)
Little's area (Kiesselbach's plexus) on the anterior nasal septum
explanation: Little's area (Kiesselbach's plexus) on the anteroinferior nasal septum is the most common site of epistaxis, accounting for approximately 90% of cases. It is a confluence of five vessels (anterior and posterior ethmoidal arteries, greater palatine, superior labial, and sphenopalatine arteries). Anterior epistaxis is typically unilateral, visible on anterior rhinoscopy, self-limiting, and more common in young patients and dry conditions. The clinical scenario with visible dilated vessels on the anterior septum is classic.
wrongAnswerExplanations: The superior turbinate region is not a common bleeding site; posterior ethmoidal artery bleeds are uncommon and typically occur in older hypertensive patients.
Posterior epistaxis involving the sphenopalatine artery tends to be profuse, bilateral, and occurs in older adults with hypertension; it is not visible on anterior rhinoscopy.
Little's area on the anterior nasal septum is the correct answer — it is the most common site of epistaxis, visible on anterior rhinoscopy, and confirmed by the clinical description.
The inferior turbinate is not a primary bleeding site; epistaxis from this area would not show prominent vessels on the anterior septum.
Posterior septal artery involvement causes posterior epistaxis — profuse, harder to visualise, and unlikely to be self-limiting in 5 minutes.
clinicalPearl: Little's area (Kiesselbach's plexus) on the anterior nasal septum is responsible for ~90% of all epistaxis cases and is the first site to inspect on anterior rhinoscopy.
keyClue: Visible dilated vessels on the anterior nasal septum on rhinoscopy directly identifies Little's area as the bleeding source.
commonExamTrap: Assuming posterior epistaxis in any recurrent nose bleed — posterior bleeds are profuse, bilateral, occur in older patients, and are NOT visible on anterior rhinoscopy.
missedConcept: Anterior vs posterior epistaxis distinction and the anatomy of Kiesselbach's plexus.

clinicalStem: A 68-year-old man with known hypertension and atrial fibrillation on warfarin is brought to the emergency department with heavy bilateral nose bleeds. He cannot identify which nostril started first. Blood is also seen dripping into his oropharynx. He appears pale, pulse 102 bpm, BP 160/95 mmHg. Anterior rhinoscopy reveals no active bleeding point. Nasal packing has been applied. Which investigation is MOST urgently required?
options: CT angiography of the carotid arteries
explanation: This patient has posterior epistaxis in the context of anticoagulation with warfarin — the INR and FBC are urgently required. An elevated INR can cause life-threatening haemorrhage and must be measured immediately to guide reversal (with vitamin K, FFP, or 4-factor PCC). FBC assesses haemodynamic impact and platelet count. Coagulation status is the key modifiable factor in this presentation. CT angiography is reserved for refractory or recurrent posterior bleeds requiring embolisation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Skin prick testing is relevant for allergic rhinitis workup and has no role in the acute management of posterior epistaxis.
CT angiography is used for planning arterial embolisation in refractory posterior epistaxis; it is not the first urgent investigation when anticoagulant status is unknown.
INR and FBC are the most urgent investigations — warfarin must be monitored and reversed if supratherapeutic, and haemoglobin quantifies blood loss — this is the correct answer.
Rigid nasal endoscopy is useful after packing to identify the bleeding site but requires stable haemodynamics and normal coagulation status first.
Sinus X-ray provides no useful information in the acute management of epistaxis.
clinicalPearl: In any patient on anticoagulants presenting with epistaxis, immediately check the INR and FBC — a supratherapeutic INR requires urgent reversal to control bleeding.
keyClue: Patient is on warfarin, has bilateral heavy bleeds with blood in the oropharynx (posterior source), and is haemodynamically compromised — coagulation status is the priority.
commonExamTrap: Jumping to CT angiography or nasal endoscopy before checking coagulation — anticoagulant reversal may be all that is needed to stop the bleed.
missedConcept: The role of coagulopathy (especially warfarin) as a treatable cause of posterior epistaxis and the urgency of INR measurement.
redFlagReason: Heavy posterior epistaxis with haemodynamic compromise (tachycardia, pallor) in an anticoagulated patient is a haemorrhagic emergency requiring urgent coagulation reversal and resuscitation.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old rugby player sustains a direct blow to the nose during a match. He attends the emergency department the same evening with nasal pain, swelling, bruising, and deformity. He can breathe through both nostrils and there is no active bleeding. Examination reveals significant perinasal oedema. He asks when he should return to confirm whether the nose is fractured and whether it needs to be straightened. What is the MOST appropriate advice?
options: Perform an urgent CT scan of the facial bones tonight
Obtain plain X-rays of the nasal bones tonight to confirm fracture
Reassess at 5–7 days post-injury when swelling has subsided
Refer immediately to maxillofacial surgery for open reduction
Arrange an MRI of the nose at 48 hours to assess soft tissue injury
explanation: In nasal fracture assessment, acute oedema obscures clinical deformity. Reassessment at 5–7 days after injury allows swelling to subside so that the true nasal contour can be assessed and any aesthetic or functional deformity identified. If manipulation is required, closed reduction under local anaesthesia is best performed at 7–14 days — before callus formation makes reduction difficult. Plain X-rays add little to clinical assessment of nasal fractures and are generally not recommended.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Urgent CT is not indicated for an isolated nasal fracture in a stable patient with no airway compromise; it exposes the patient to unnecessary radiation.
Plain X-rays of the nasal bones have poor sensitivity and specificity for nasal fractures and do not change acute management; they are not recommended as routine.
Reassessment at 5–7 days when swelling subsides is the standard approach — it allows accurate deformity assessment and timely manipulation if needed — this is the correct answer.
Open reduction is reserved for complex mid-face fractures; isolated nasal fractures are managed conservatively or with closed reduction.
MRI has no role in isolated nasal fracture assessment; it is not used for bony injuries in this context.
clinicalPearl: Reassess nasal fractures at 5–7 days when acute oedema resolves; closed reduction should be performed at 7–14 days before fibrous union occurs.
keyClue: Acute perinasal oedema obscures deformity — the correct timing for reassessment and reduction planning is 5–7 days post-injury.
commonExamTrap: Ordering plain X-rays — they rarely change management for isolated nasal fractures and are not recommended in most guidelines.
missedConcept: The optimal timing window for nasal fracture manipulation (7–14 days) and that acute assessment is unreliable due to swelling.

clinicalStem: A 16-year-old boy is seen in the emergency department following a fist fight. He sustained a blow to the nose 2 hours ago. He complains of complete bilateral nasal obstruction and nasal pain. On examination there is mild external bruising. Anterior rhinoscopy reveals a smooth, fluctuant, bilateral swelling of the nasal septum that is tender to touch. The swelling obliterates both nasal passages. Which is the MOST likely diagnosis?
explanation: A nasal septal haematoma presents after nasal trauma as a smooth, fluctuant, bilateral septal swelling that is tender and obliterates the nasal airway. Blood collects between the septal cartilage and the overlying perichondrium. Without urgent incision and drainage, the cartilage loses its blood supply (avascular necrosis) leading to saddle-nose deformity. It can also become infected, forming a septal abscess. Nasal polyps are non-tender and typically present without acute trauma.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Nasal bone fracture causes external bony deformity and tenderness but does not produce a smooth, fluctuant, bilateral intranasal swelling obliterating the airway.
Acute bacterial rhinosinusitis presents with facial pain/pressure, congestion, and purulent discharge — not a fluctuant intranasal mass after acute trauma.
Nasal polyps are pale, soft, non-tender masses arising from the middle meatus; they are not related to acute trauma and do not cause bilateral fluctuant swelling.
Nasal septal haematoma perfectly matches: post-traumatic, bilateral, fluctuant, tender, smooth septal swelling obliterating the airway — this is the correct answer.
A deviated nasal septum causes unilateral obstruction and is a chronic finding; it is not fluctuant and does not present acutely after trauma.
clinicalPearl: A fluctuant bilateral septal swelling after nasal trauma is a septal haematoma — it requires urgent incision and drainage to prevent avascular necrosis and saddle-nose deformity.
keyClue: Smooth, fluctuant, bilateral septal swelling that is tender after nasal trauma — fluctuance (fluid-filled collection) is the key diagnostic clue.
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing it as a nasal fracture only — a haematoma must be actively excluded after any nasal trauma as missing it leads to a preventable complication.
missedConcept: Nasal septal haematoma is a surgical emergency requiring same-day drainage; untreated, it causes cartilage necrosis and saddle-nose deformity.
redFlagReason: Untreated nasal septal haematoma causes avascular necrosis of the septal cartilage and permanent saddle-nose deformity; it can also progress to a septal abscess with intracranial spread (cavernous sinus thrombosis).

clinicalStem: A 22-year-old competitive swimmer presents with a 1-week history of left ear pain, discharge, and itch. The pain worsens markedly when she chews or when the tragus is pressed. She has been swimming daily. Examination reveals erythema and oedema of the external auditory canal with a creamy discharge. The tympanic membrane is intact and normal. Audiometry shows no conductive hearing loss. Which is the MOST likely causative organism?
explanation: Pseudomonas aeruginosa is the most common causative organism in acute otitis externa (swimmer's ear), accounting for approximately 60% of cases, followed by Staphylococcus aureus (~30%). The moist, alkaline environment created by repeated water exposure disrupts the protective cerumen layer and promotes Pseudomonas colonisation. Tragus tenderness and pain on jaw movement due to anterior wall proximity are hallmarks of otitis externa. Fungal (Aspergillus, Candida) otitis externa is much less common and typically follows antibiotic use.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Streptococcus pneumoniae is a common cause of acute otitis media (middle ear), not otitis externa.
Pseudomonas aeruginosa is the most common organism in acute otitis externa, especially in swimmers — this is the correct answer.
Candida albicans causes fungal otitis externa (otomycosis), typically after prolonged antibiotic use; it is much less common than bacterial causes in this context.
Staphylococcus aureus is the second most common cause of otitis externa but is less prevalent than Pseudomonas, especially in swimmers.
Aspergillus niger causes otomycosis and presents with black/grey spores visible in the canal; it is not the predominant organism in swimmer's ear.
clinicalPearl: Pseudomonas aeruginosa is the most common cause of acute bacterial otitis externa ('swimmer's ear'); the hallmark sign is tragus tenderness.
keyClue: Daily swimming + creamy discharge + tragus tenderness = classic acute otitis externa; swimming provides the moist alkaline environment favoured by Pseudomonas.
commonExamTrap: Choosing Staphylococcus aureus — it is the second most common but Pseudomonas predominates in swimmer's ear.
missedConcept: Otitis externa vs otitis media distinction (intact vs perforated TM, tragus tenderness vs Valsalva test) and the role of swimming in predisposing to Pseudomonas.

clinicalStem: A 71-year-old man with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 98 mmol/mol) presents with severe, constant left ear pain that is worse at night and not responding to topical antibiotics. He has a profuse purulent left ear discharge. Examination reveals granulation tissue on the floor of the external auditory canal at the bony-cartilaginous junction. Cranial nerve VII palsy is also noted on the left. His CRP is 210 mg/L and WBC 14 × 10⁹/L. Which SINGLE investigation is MOST appropriate to confirm the diagnosis and assess disease extent?
options: Swab of the ear discharge for culture
explanation: Malignant (necrotising) otitis externa (MOE) is an invasive osteomyelitis of the skull base caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa, predominantly in elderly diabetic or immunocompromised patients. The hallmarks are severe pain disproportionate to local signs, granulation tissue at the bony-cartilaginous junction, and cranial nerve involvement (VII most commonly). MRI with gadolinium is the gold-standard for assessing soft-tissue involvement, dural enhancement, and intracranial extension. CT is better for bony erosion but MRI provides superior soft-tissue detail for staging. Both are often used together, but MRI with gadolinium best assesses disease extent.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Pure tone audiogram assesses hearing function but provides no information about disease extent, bone destruction, or intracranial involvement.
Temporal bone MRI with gadolinium is the best investigation for assessing soft-tissue extent, skull base involvement, and intracranial spread in malignant otitis externa — this is the correct answer.
Ear discharge swab identifies the causative organism (Pseudomonas) and guides antibiotic therapy but does not assess disease extent or confirm skull base osteomyelitis.
CT head without contrast may show early bony erosion but is inferior to MRI with gadolinium for soft-tissue staging and assessing intracranial involvement.
Tympanometry assesses middle ear pressure and is entirely irrelevant in this context of skull base osteomyelitis.
clinicalPearl: Malignant otitis externa in a diabetic patient: granulation tissue at the EAC floor + CN VII palsy = skull base osteomyelitis; MRI with gadolinium is the investigation of choice for staging.
keyClue: Granulation tissue at the bony-cartilaginous junction of the EAC plus cranial nerve VII palsy in a diabetic patient = malignant otitis externa (skull base osteomyelitis).
commonExamTrap: Ordering only a CT without contrast — CT is good for bony erosion but MRI with gadolinium provides superior soft-tissue and intracranial assessment.
missedConcept: Malignant otitis externa is not a simple ear infection — it is skull base osteomyelitis requiring prolonged anti-pseudomonal therapy and advanced imaging for staging.
redFlagReason: Malignant otitis externa with cranial nerve involvement signifies skull base osteomyelitis with risk of intracranial extension, further cranial nerve palsies, meningitis, and death; delayed diagnosis is life-threatening.

topic: Exostoses vs osteoma of external auditory canal
clinicalStem: A 35-year-old male surfer presents with progressive bilateral conductive hearing loss and recurrent episodes of otitis externa. He has been surfing in cold seawater for 15 years. Otoscopy reveals multiple smooth, broad-based, bilateral bony swellings in the medial external auditory canal that partially occlude the lumen. The tympanic membranes are intact. A colleague suggests the diagnosis, but an ENT registrar disagrees, noting a different bony lesion could explain it. Which features BEST differentiate exostoses from osteoma of the external auditory canal?
options: Exostoses are pedunculated and unilateral; osteoma is broad-based and bilateral
Exostoses are associated with prolonged cold water exposure and are bilateral and broad-based; osteoma is pedunculated, typically unilateral, and not associated with cold water
Exostoses are cystic and pain-free; osteoma is solid and causes severe otalgia
Exostoses arise from the cartilaginous canal; osteoma arises from the bony canal
Exostoses are caused by bacterial infection; osteoma is a congenital lesion
explanation: Exostoses of the external auditory canal are benign multiple, bilateral, broad-based bony overgrowths of the bony EAC caused by repeated cold water exposure (surfer's ear, diver's ear). They are the most common benign bony lesion of the EAC. Osteoma is a solitary, pedunculated, unilateral bony lesion arising at the tympanosquamous or tympanomastoid suture line and is not related to cold water exposure. This distinction determines surgical approach and patient counselling.
wrongAnswerExplanations: This is inverted — exostoses are broad-based and bilateral; osteoma is the pedunculated, typically unilateral lesion.

This correctly describes the distinguishing features — exostoses are bilateral, broad-based, cold water-associated; osteoma is pedunculated, unilateral, and unrelated to cold water — this is the correct answer.
Neither lesion is cystic; both are solid bony growths. Osteoma does not typically cause severe otalgia.
Both exostoses and osteoma arise from the bony external auditory canal, not the cartilaginous portion.
Neither is caused by bacterial infection; exostoses are a reactive response to cold water exposure and osteoma is a benign bone-forming tumour.
clinicalPearl: Surfer's ear = exostoses: bilateral, broad-based, multiple bony swellings of the bony EAC caused by cold water exposure; osteoma is pedunculated, unilateral, and incidental.
keyClue: Bilateral, broad-based bony swellings in a long-term cold-water surfer = exostoses (surfer's ear).
commonExamTrap: Confusing exostoses with osteoma — the key distinguishing features are laterality, morphology (broad-based vs pedunculated), and the history of cold water exposure.
missedConcept: The aetiological and morphological distinction between EAC exostoses and osteoma, and their different clinical contexts.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old woman presents to her GP with a 4-month history of nasal blockage, thick discoloured nasal discharge, facial heaviness, and reduced smell. She reports worsening symptoms despite two short courses of antibiotics. She denies any recent upper respiratory tract infection. Examination reveals anterior rhinoscopy with mucopurulent discharge but no visible polyps. Which combination of findings BEST satisfies the diagnostic criteria for chronic rhinosinusitis (CRS)?
options: Single symptom of nasal obstruction lasting more than 4 weeks with normal rhinoscopy
At least two symptoms including nasal blockage or discharge, lasting 12 or more weeks, with endoscopic or CT evidence of sinonasal disease
Four or more episodes of acute sinusitis per year, regardless of symptom duration between episodes
Positive culture of Staphylococcus aureus from nasal swab with 4 weeks of symptoms
Facial pain with purulent nasal discharge lasting more than 2 weeks with elevated CRP
explanation: The EPOS (European Position Paper on Rhinosinusitis and Nasal Polyps) diagnostic criteria for CRS require: two or more symptoms (one of which must be nasal blockage/obstruction or nasal discharge), persisting for 12 or more weeks, with objective evidence of disease on nasal endoscopy (mucosal oedema, polyps, or discharge from the middle meatus) OR CT of the sinuses (mucosal changes within the ostiomeatal complex or sinuses). Symptom duration of less than 12 weeks defines acute or subacute rhinosinusitis. A bacterial culture alone is not a diagnostic criterion.
wrongAnswerExplanations: A single symptom lasting 4 weeks does not meet CRS criteria; at least two symptoms for at least 12 weeks with objective evidence are required.
This correctly states the EPOS CRS criteria: two or more symptoms (including blockage or discharge) for ≥12 weeks with objective endoscopic or CT evidence — this is the correct answer.
Recurrent acute rhinosinusitis (RARS) is defined as four or more episodes per year with symptom-free intervals; this is a separate entity from CRS.
A positive nasal swab culture is not a diagnostic criterion for CRS; microbiological confirmation is not required.
Elevated CRP with 2 weeks of purulent discharge and facial pain is more consistent with acute bacterial rhinosinusitis, not CRS.
clinicalPearl: CRS is diagnosed by ≥2 symptoms (including blockage or discharge) for ≥12 weeks with objective evidence on endoscopy or CT — not by symptoms alone or by bacterial culture.
keyClue: Four months (>12 weeks) of nasal blockage, discoloured discharge, facial heaviness, and reduced smell with mucopurulent discharge on rhinoscopy satisfies EPOS CRS criteria.
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing CRS based on symptoms alone without objective evidence, or confusing recurrent acute sinusitis with CRS.
missedConcept: The EPOS diagnostic criteria for CRS require two criteria: symptom duration ≥12 weeks AND objective evidence (endoscopy or CT) — symptoms alone are insufficient.
